does: This paper develops a comprehensive architectural framework for understanding AI safety by organizing it across three interconnected dimensions: Trustworthy AI (addressing technical reliability and robustness), Responsible AI (handling fairness, transparency, and accountability), and Safe AI (mitigating harmful outputs and misuse). The authors conduct an extensive review of current research and state-of-the-art techniques in each dimension, with particular focus on Large Language Models as a key application area. They catalog key challenges specific to LLMs such as hallucinations, prompt injection, jailbreaking, and bias, while surveying existing mitigation approaches including alignment techniques, safety training, monitoring systems, and evaluation frameworks. The paper synthesizes innovative mechanisms and methodologies for designing and testing safety properties across the three perspectives.
connects: This work builds on decades of AI safety research spanning from early formal verification methods in autonomous systems to recent work on alignment and value learning in neural networks. It extends prior taxonomies of AI safety concerns by incorporating insights from responsible AI and trustworthy AI movements that emerged in response to real-world harms from deployed systems. The paper particularly draws on recent advances in LLM safety research including work on constitutional AI, reinforcement learning from human feedback, adversarial testing, and interpretability methods. It also connects to broader literature on fairness in machine learning, robustness against distribution shift, adversarial examples, and the technical governance of AI systems. The three-perspective framework synthesizes contributions from the AI ethics, ML security, and formal AI safety communities.
enables: This taxonomy and framework should facilitate more coordinated research across fragmented AI safety communities by providing common language and organization principles. The structured analysis of current techniques and gaps opens pathways for developing more rigorous methods for safety evaluation and certification of LLMs at scale. By articulating challenges across trustworthiness, responsibility, and safety dimensions, the work enables researchers to identify overlooked interactions between these perspectives and design integrated safety solutions rather than treating them separately. The survey of state-of-the-art mechanisms provides a foundation for building more sophisticated safety architectures combining multiple techniques. Ultimately, this framework could support the development of safety standards, benchmarks, and regulatory approaches for evaluating and governing large language models as they become more central to critical applications.
